planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost risk has passed; a large, golden-orange beefsteak heirloom with very low acid and sweet, fruity flavor — an excellent choice for gardeners who want an orange type that ripens reliably in hot summer conditions.

directions: Start seed at 70–80°F 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant into rich, deeply prepared, well-drained soil after all frost risk has passed, burying the stem deeply and staking or caging firmly. Persimmon is a Ukrainian heirloom beefsteak tomato with fruit that resembles the color of a ripe persimmon — vivid golden-orange — with very meaty flesh, a small seed cavity, extraordinarily sweet, low-acid, fruity flavor, and fruits weighing 10–16 ounces; it is among the finest-tasting orange or golden beefsteak heirlooms and has been prized by serious tomato growers since its introduction through Seed Savers Exchange. The vigorous indeterminate vines produce steadily through the season; stake firmly and maintain consistent moisture to prevent blossom-end rot. Harvest when fruits are fully colored and yield slightly to pressure at the blossom end.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; a reliably productive red beefsteak heirloom with excellent disease resistance that performs consistently in both humid and dry climates — a good choice for beginners seeking a dependable large heirloom tomato.

directions: Start seed at 70–80°F 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost risk has passed into deeply prepared, fertile, well-drained soil. Box Car Willie is an American heirloom red beefsteak tomato named with Old West flair and popular for its reliable productivity, large 8–14 ounce fruits with smooth, meaty red flesh and excellent balanced sweet-acid flavor, and its relative disease resistance compared to many other heirlooms — it typically outproduces more famous heirlooms like Brandywine under variable conditions. The vigorous, indeterminate vines need staking or caging; fruits are round to slightly flattened with smooth, crack-resistant skin. A solid everyday heirloom choice for gardeners who want large, flavorful tomatoes without the fussiness of some heritage types.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; a large bicolor beefsteak with stunning golden-orange skin blushed red and equally beautiful red-and-gold marbled flesh — one of the most visually dramatic heirloom tomatoes available.

directions: Start seed at 70–80°F 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant into deeply amended, well-drained soil after all frost risk has passed and stake or cage firmly to support the large, heavy fruits. Lucky Cross is an exquisite heirloom bicolor tomato producing large, 1–2 pound beefsteak fruits with golden-yellow to orange skin blushed with a red blush at the blossom end and extraordinarily beautiful red-and-gold marbled flesh that is as ornamental sliced on a plate as it is delicious — the flavor is sweet, low-acid, and tropically fruity with exceptional richness. Its rarity and stunning appearance make it a prize at farmers' markets and seed saving circles. The indeterminate vines are vigorous and productive; harvest when fruit softens at the blossom end and the skin transitions fully from green.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; Omar Arab is a Middle Eastern heirloom with elongated, pleated, deep red fruits that are outstanding for stuffing whole in the traditional Levantine style; well-adapted to hot, dry summer conditions and therefore an excellent choice for zone 8–11.

directions: Start seed at 70–80°F 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant to fertile, well-drained soil after all frost risk has passed. Omar Arab is a Syrian heirloom tomato with elongated, ribbed or lobed, deep red to crimson fruits 8–12 ounces in weight with a distinctive beaked tip and meaty, near-seedless flesh with very few gel pockets that makes it outstanding for traditional Levantine-style stuffed tomatoes (mahashi), where the tomato is cored, filled, and cooked whole; the balanced, savory-sweet flavor is excellent fresh as well. Adapted to hot, dry Mediterranean-type climates, it performs reliably in zone 8–11 high-summer conditions where other heirlooms drop flowers or crack. Seed was collected and introduced more widely through the work of heritage seed savers in the Arab world.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; an Appalachian family heirloom from West Virginia producing extraordinarily large and meaty orange beefsteak fruits — a lesser-known gem introduced through Seed Savers Exchange that rivals better-known orange types in flavor and productivity.

directions: Sow seed at 70–80°F 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant into deeply prepared, fertile, well-drained soil after all frost danger has passed and stake or cage firmly from the start. Vinson Watts is a West Virginia Appalachian family heirloom that arrived at Seed Savers Exchange through Vinson Watts, who grew it for many years before contributing it to the seed exchange; it produces very large, 1–2 pound orange beefsteak fruits with deep, meaty, mostly seedless flesh and a mild, sweet, low-acid flavor that rivals the better-known Kellogg's Breakfast and Dr. Wyche's Orange. The vigorous indeterminate vines are productive and the fruits have a pleasing globe shape and smooth skin that resists cracking better than some large heirlooms. An excellent choice for anyone building a collection of orange heirloom beefsteak tomatoes.
